column8: Why is an optimal level important?
column9:
Optimal triglycerides under 80 mg/dL indicate efficient fat metabolism and low metabolic risk.
 
 Triglycerides are extremely responsive to reducing refined carbohydrates, sugar, and alcohol—often improving dramatically within weeks.

column5:
ALT (alanine aminotransferase) is an enzyme found mainly in your liver. When liver cells are damaged, ALT leaks into your blood.
 
 It's one of the most specific markers for liver health.
column7:
Elevated ALT indicates liver stress—from fatty liver, hepatitis, medications, or other causes. Even levels at the high end of 'normal' may indicate early fatty liver.
 
 Optimal: under 25 U/L for men, under 19 U/L for women (stricter than standard ranges).
column8: Why is an optimal level important?
column9:
Optimal ALT in the lower portion of normal indicates healthy liver cells without ongoing damage.
 
 ALT often improves quickly with lifestyle changes—reducing alcohol, losing weight, and improving diet.
column11:
ALT (alanine aminotransferase) is an enzyme found mainly in your liver. When liver cells are damaged, ALT leaks into your blood.
 
 Men typically have slightly higher ALT than women.
column13:
Elevated ALT indicates liver stress. For men, the standard 'normal' range goes up to 56 U/L, but optimal is under 25 U/L.
 
 Even levels in the 30s may indicate early fatty liver disease.
column14: Why is an optimal level important?
column15:
For men, optimal ALT under 25 U/L indicates healthy liver tissue without significant fat accumulation or damage.
 
 ALT responds well to reducing alcohol, losing excess weight, and addressing fatty liver.
column17:
ALT (alanine aminotransferase) is an enzyme found mainly in your liver. When liver cells are damaged, ALT leaks into your blood.
 
 Women typically have lower ALT than men.
column19:
Elevated ALT indicates liver stress. For women, the standard 'normal' range goes up to 40 U/L, but optimal is under 19 U/L.
 
 Even levels in the 20s may warrant attention.
column20: Why is an optimal level important?
column21:
For women, optimal ALT under 19 U/L indicates healthy liver tissue without significant fat accumulation or damage.
 
 ALT responds well to lifestyle changes—weight management, reducing alcohol, and improving diet.

column5:
AST (aspartate aminotransferase) is an enzyme found in your liver, heart, and muscles. When these tissues are damaged, AST leaks into your blood.
 
 Because it's not specific to the liver, it's usually interpreted alongside ALT.
column7:
Elevated AST with elevated ALT usually indicates liver problems. Elevated AST with normal ALT might be from muscle damage, intense exercise, or heart issues.
 
 The AST/ALT ratio (Liver Health Ratio) helps distinguish different causes.
column8: Why is an optimal level important?
column9:
Optimal AST in the lower-normal range indicates healthy liver, muscle, and heart tissue.
 
 If AST is elevated but ALT is normal, consider recent strenuous exercise as a possible cause before assuming liver problems.

column5:
Creatinine is a waste product from muscle metabolism. Your kidneys filter it out at a steady rate, making it a reliable marker of kidney function.
 
 eGFR (estimated glomerular filtration rate) converts creatinine into an estimate of how well your kidneys are filtering.
column7:
High creatinine/low eGFR suggests your kidneys aren't filtering well. This can be from kidney disease, dehydration, or certain medications.
 
 eGFR above 90 is normal. 60-89 may indicate mild kidney dysfunction. Below 60 warrants investigation.
column8: Why is an optimal level important?
column9:
Optimal eGFR above 90 mL/min indicates your kidneys are efficiently filtering waste from your blood.
 
 Protecting kidney function is important—staying hydrated, managing blood pressure, and avoiding nephrotoxic medications helps preserve kidney health.
column11:
Creatinine is a waste product from muscle metabolism. Men typically have higher creatinine than women due to greater muscle mass.
 
 eGFR adjusts for this, giving a standardized measure of kidney function.
column13:
For men, higher creatinine is expected due to muscle mass. eGFR above 90 is normal. Below 60 indicates significant kidney dysfunction.
 
 Muscle-building supplements containing creatine can raise creatinine without affecting actual kidney function.
column14: Why is an optimal level important?
column15:
For men, optimal eGFR above 90 mL/min indicates healthy kidney filtration.
 
 Stay hydrated, manage blood pressure, and be cautious with supplements and medications that can affect kidney function.
column17:
Creatinine is a waste product from muscle metabolism. Women typically have lower creatinine than men due to less muscle mass.
 
 eGFR adjusts for this, giving a standardized measure of kidney function.
column19:
For women, even mildly elevated creatinine may be significant. eGFR above 90 is normal. Below 60 indicates significant kidney dysfunction.
 
 eGFR naturally declines somewhat with age.
column20: Why is an optimal level important?
column21:
For women, optimal eGFR above 90 mL/min indicates healthy kidney filtration.
 
 Protecting kidney function is especially important with age—stay hydrated and manage blood pressure.

column5:
High-sensitivity CRP (C-reactive protein) is made by your liver in response to inflammation. It's a general marker of inflammation anywhere in your body.
 
 The 'high-sensitivity' version can detect lower levels, important for cardiovascular risk assessment.
column7:
Elevated hs-CRP is associated with cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and other chronic conditions. It's an independent risk factor for heart disease.
 
 Optimal: under 1.0 mg/L. 1-3 is average risk. Above 3 is elevated risk (unless you have an acute infection).
column8: Why is an optimal level important?
column9:
Optimal hs-CRP under 0.5 mg/L indicates minimal systemic inflammation and lower cardiovascular risk.
 
 CRP responds to lifestyle factors—weight loss, exercise, omega-3s, and reducing sugar can all lower it.

column5:
Cortisol is your body's main stress hormone, produced by your adrenal glands. It follows a daily rhythm—highest in the morning, lowest at night.
 
 It helps regulate metabolism, immune function, and the stress response.
column7:
High cortisol can indicate chronic stress, Cushing's syndrome, or just a stressful morning. Low cortisol might suggest adrenal insufficiency.
 
 Timing matters—morning cortisol should be measured within an hour of waking for accurate interpretation.
column8: Why is an optimal level important?
column9:
Optimal morning cortisol indicates healthy adrenal function and appropriate stress response capability.
 
 Chronic elevation from ongoing stress can be addressed through stress management, sleep optimization, and sometimes adaptogenic supplements.

column5:
Serum iron measures the amount of iron circulating in your blood. Iron is essential for carrying oxygen in red blood cells and for energy production.
 
 Serum iron fluctuates daily and after meals, so it's usually interpreted with other iron markers.
column7:
Low iron can cause fatigue, weakness, and anemia. High iron (iron overload) can damage organs over time.
 
 Serum iron alone isn't enough—you need ferritin and TIBC to understand your full iron status.
column8: Why is an optimal level important?
column9:
Optimal serum iron indicates adequate iron for oxygen transport and energy production.
 
 Interpret iron alongside ferritin and TIBC for the complete picture—all three together reveal whether you're deficient, optimal, or overloaded.
column11:
Serum iron measures the iron circulating in your blood. Men typically have higher iron stores than women (no monthly iron loss through menstruation).
 
 Iron is essential for oxygen transport and energy production.
column13:
Low iron causes fatigue and anemia. But men are more at risk for iron overload—a condition that causes organ damage over time.
 
 Men with high iron should be screened for hemochromatosis (genetic iron overload).
column14: Why is an optimal level important?
column15:
For men, optimal iron supports energy without risking iron overload. Ferritin is a better marker of stores.
 
 If ferritin is high (above 300 ng/mL), investigate further—hemochromatosis is common and treatable.
column17:
Serum iron measures the iron circulating in your blood. Women of reproductive age are more prone to iron deficiency due to menstrual blood loss.
 
 Iron is essential for oxygen transport and energy production.
column19:
Low iron is one of the most common causes of fatigue in women. Heavy periods increase the risk.
 
 Iron needs change with menstrual status, pregnancy, and menopause.
column20: Why is an optimal level important?
column21:
For women, optimal iron prevents the fatigue and other symptoms of deficiency that are so common.
 
 If periods are heavy, proactive iron monitoring and supplementation may be needed to maintain optimal levels.

column5:
Ferritin is your body's iron storage protein. It reflects how much iron you have in reserve—your body's iron bank account.
 
 It's the most useful single marker for assessing iron status.
column7:
Low ferritin causes fatigue and eventually anemia—often before other iron markers change. High ferritin can indicate iron overload or inflammation.
 
 Optimal: 50-150 ng/mL for most people. Athletes and menstruating women may need higher levels.
column8: Why is an optimal level important?
column9:
Optimal ferritin ensures adequate iron reserves for energy, exercise recovery, and immune function.
 
 Low ferritin is easily treated with iron supplements. High ferritin needs investigation—it could be inflammation or iron overload.
column11:
Ferritin is your body's iron storage protein. Men typically have higher ferritin than women and rarely become deficient without blood loss.
 
 It's the best single marker for iron status.
column13:
For men, the bigger concern is often iron overload, not deficiency. Ferritin above 300 ng/mL warrants investigation for hemochromatosis.
 
 Optimal for men: 50-150 ng/mL. Low ferritin causes fatigue; high ferritin can damage organs.
column14: Why is an optimal level important?
column15:
For men, optimal ferritin (50-150 ng/mL) supports energy without risking iron overload.
 
 If ferritin is high, genetic testing for hemochromatosis is reasonable—it's common (1 in 200 people) and very treatable with regular blood donation.
column17:
Ferritin is your body's iron storage protein. Women of reproductive age commonly have low ferritin due to menstrual blood loss.
 
 It's the most useful marker for iron status.
column19:
Low ferritin is extremely common in women and causes fatigue, hair loss, poor exercise tolerance, and eventually anemia.
 
 Optimal for women: 50-150 ng/mL. Many women function best above 70 ng/mL.
column20: Why is an optimal level important?
column21:
For women, optimal ferritin (50-150 ng/mL) supports energy, hair health, exercise performance, and overall vitality.
 
 If periods are heavy or you're experiencing fatigue or hair loss, checking and optimizing ferritin often helps dramatically.

column5:
Vitamin D is actually a hormone that your skin produces from sunlight. It's essential for bone health, immune function, mood, and much more.
 
 Deficiency is extremely common, especially in places where people avoid sun exposure.
column7:
Low vitamin D is linked to osteoporosis, depression, immune dysfunction, and increased risk of various diseases.
 
 Optimal: 40-60 ng/mL. Many people are deficient despite living in tropical climates—indoor lifestyles and sun avoidance are common causes.
column8: Why is an optimal level important?
column9:
Optimal vitamin D (40-60 ng/mL) supports bone health, immune function, mood, and may reduce risk of various diseases.
 
 Most people need supplementation to reach optimal levels, especially with indoor lifestyles. Testing guides appropriate dosing.

column5:
Free testosterone is the small fraction (2-3%) of testosterone that isn't bound to proteins. It's the testosterone that's actually available for your tissues to use.
 
 The rest is bound to SHBG (tightly) or albumin (loosely).
column7:
You can have normal total testosterone but low free testosterone—explaining symptoms despite 'normal' levels.
 
 Free testosterone is particularly useful when SHBG is abnormal, as it shows what's actually bioavailable.
column8: Why is an optimal level important?
column9:
Optimal free testosterone ensures adequate hormone actually reaches your tissues for its effects on energy, mood, muscle, and sexual function.
 
 If free testosterone is low despite normal total, the focus shifts to factors affecting SHBG.
column11:
Free testosterone is the 2-3% of testosterone not bound to proteins—it's what's actually available for your tissues to use.
 
 Total testosterone can look normal while free testosterone is low, especially if SHBG is elevated.
column13:
For men, optimal free testosterone is typically 9-30 ng/dL. If you have symptoms like fatigue or low libido despite 'normal' total testosterone, low free testosterone may be the issue.
 
 Free testosterone is what your cells actually respond to.
column14: Why is an optimal level important?
column15:
For men, optimal free testosterone (15-25 ng/dL) ensures adequate hormone for energy, mood, muscle mass, and sexual function.
 
 If low despite normal total testosterone, addressing SHBG (through thyroid optimization, liver health, body composition) may help.
column17:
Free testosterone is the small fraction of testosterone not bound to proteins. For women, even tiny amounts of free testosterone matter for energy and libido.
 
 It represents what's actually available to your tissues.
column19:
For women, optimal free testosterone is typically 0.3-1.9 ng/dL. Low levels can cause fatigue and low libido. High levels may indicate PCOS.
 
 It's useful when symptoms don't match total testosterone levels.
column20: Why is an optimal level important?
column21:
For women, optimal free testosterone supports energy, libido, and vitality without excess androgenic effects.
 
 Both low and high levels are actionable—low may respond to DHEA; high warrants PCOS evaluation.

column5:
SHBG (sex hormone-binding globulin) is a protein that binds testosterone and estrogen, controlling how much is 'free' and active. High SHBG means less free hormone.
 
 SHBG is produced by the liver and responds to various factors.
column7:
High SHBG can make you functionally low in testosterone even with normal total levels. Low SHBG can mean too much free hormone.
 
 Factors that raise SHBG: aging, hyperthyroidism, low body weight. Factors that lower it: obesity, insulin resistance, hypothyroidism.
column8: Why is an optimal level important?
column9:
Optimal SHBG allows appropriate amounts of free hormones to reach your tissues.
 
 If SHBG is too high or low, addressing the underlying cause (thyroid, insulin resistance, body composition) often helps normalize it.
column11:
SHBG (sex hormone-binding globulin) binds testosterone, controlling how much is 'free' and active. High SHBG means less bioavailable testosterone.
 
 SHBG increases with age, thyroid hormone, and low body weight. It decreases with obesity and insulin resistance.
column13:
For men, high SHBG can cause low testosterone symptoms despite normal total testosterone. Normal male SHBG: 10-57 nmol/L.
 
 If SHBG is high, increasing testosterone or addressing the cause (thyroid, body composition) may help.
column14: Why is an optimal level important?
column15:
For men, optimal SHBG (20-40 nmol/L) allows adequate free testosterone while providing appropriate regulation.
 
 Very low SHBG (from insulin resistance) or very high SHBG both cause problems. Addressing the underlying cause is key.
column17:
SHBG (sex hormone-binding globulin) binds both testosterone and estrogen, controlling how much is 'free' and active.
 
 Women typically have higher SHBG than men, especially on oral contraceptives or HRT.
column19:
For women, low SHBG (from insulin resistance or PCOS) can mean too much free testosterone, causing acne and unwanted hair growth. Very high SHBG can cause low libido.
 
 Normal female SHBG: 18-144 nmol/L (varies widely).
column20: Why is an optimal level important?
column21:
For women, optimal SHBG maintains hormonal balance—enough free testosterone for energy and libido, but not so much that it causes androgenic symptoms.
 
 If SHBG is low (with PCOS features), improving insulin sensitivity often helps.

column5:
IGF-1 (insulin-like growth factor 1) is produced mainly by your liver in response to growth hormone. It's the main mediator of growth hormone's effects in your body.
 
 It influences muscle growth, bone density, and metabolism.
column7:
Low IGF-1 can cause fatigue, muscle loss, and poor recovery. Very high IGF-1 has been linked to accelerated aging and cancer risk in some studies.
 
 The sweet spot appears to be moderate-to-optimal levels for your age—neither too low nor too high.
column8: Why is an optimal level important?
column9:
Optimal IGF-1 for your age supports muscle maintenance, recovery, and metabolic health without the downsides of excess growth signaling.
 
 IGF-1 responds to protein intake, sleep, and exercise. Optimization focuses on lifestyle rather than supplementation in most cases.
